items.title: anatomical classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification: central vs branch

  • Retinal vein occlusion is divided into central (at the level of the optic nerve) and branch (in a venous bifurcation, more common in the superior temporal quadrant).
  • Branch occlusion usually occurs in pathological arteriovenous crossing (sclerotic artery compresses the vein). underlying).
  • Central occlusion can be ischemic (with relative afferent pupillary defect and poor visual acuity <20/200) or non-ischemic (better prognosis).

items.title: High blood pressure
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: high blood pressure

  • High blood pressure is the main risk factor for retinal vein occlusion, especially branch vein occlusion.
  • High blood pressure produces thickening of the arteriolar wall (sclerosis) that compresses the vein at the arteriovenous junctions, favoring thrombosis.
  • Control blood pressure <140/90 mmHg reduces the risk of recurrence and occlusion in the contralateral eye.

items.title: Diabetes mellitus
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: diabetes mellitus

  • Diabetes mellitus is an independent risk factor for retinal vein occlusion, both central and branch.
  • Chronic hyperglycemia induces endothelial dysfunction and hemorrheological alterations (increased blood viscosity and platelet aggregation).
  • The coexistence of diabetes mellitus and arterial hypertension multiplies the risk; It is recommended to optimize glycated hemoglobin <7% for secondary prevention.

items.title: Glaucoma
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: glaucoma

  • Glaucoma (especially open angle) is a well-established risk factor for central retinal vein occlusion.
  • The increased intraocular pressure mechanically compresses the central retinal vein as it passes through the lamina cribrosa, reducing flow and promoting thrombosis.
  • It has been reported that up to 30% of patients with central retinal vein occlusion present with glaucoma or ocular hypertension; Intraocular pressure should be measured in all patients with retinal vein occlusion.

items.title: Painless visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Typical presentation: painless visual loss

  • The classic presentation is painless visual loss and unilateral, of sudden onset (central occlusion) or more gradual (branch occlusion).
  • Visual acuity can vary from normal vision to light perception; in ischemic central occlusion it is usually <20/400.
  • There is no ocular pain or redness; The absence of pain helps differentiate it from inflammatory optic neuropathy or acute glaucoma.

items.title: Flame hemorrhages
items.contentHtml:
Fundoscopic finding: flame hemorrhages

  • Flame hemorrhages are the most characteristic funduscopic finding of retinal vein occlusion; They are located in the nerve fiber layer, following the orientation of the axons.
  • In central occlusion, flame hemorrhages are distributed in the four quadrants of the retina, with venous congestion and vascular tortuosity.
  • In branch occlusion, flame hemorrhages are limited to the drainage territory of the occluded vein (typically sectoral).

items.foco: Sign of branch occlusion: Roth's spot
items.title: Roth stain
items.contentHtml:
Branch occlusion sign: Roth spot

  • The Roth spot is a retinal hemorrhage with a white center (made up of fibrin or platelet aggregates), classically associated with infective endocarditis, but can also be observed in branch retinal vein occlusion.
  • It is not pathognomonic of retinal vein occlusion; Its presence requires ruling out endocarditis, severe anemia, leukemia and other vasculopathies.
  • In the context of branch retinal vein occlusion, Roth's spot indicates deep hemorrhage with fibrinoid exudation in the center.

items.title: macular edema
items.contentHtml:
Complication: macular edema

  • Macular edema is the most common cause of visual loss in retinal vein occlusion, both central and branch, and can appear days or weeks after the event.
  • It is caused by rupture of the blood-retinal barrier and leakage of fluid into the extracellular space of the macular retina; confirmed with optical coherence tomography (macular thickening >300 µm).
  • First-line treatment is intravitreal injection of anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF); Laser photocoagulation has been relegated to refractory cases.

items.title: Retinal neovascularization
items.contentHtml:
Complication: retinal neovascularization

  • Retinal neovascularization is a late (2-6 months) complication of ischemic retinal vein occlusion, induced by the release of vascular endothelial growth factor by the hypoxic retina.
  • The neovessels are fragile and can bleed, causing vitreous hemorrhage (sudden visual loss and myodesopsias).
  • Treatment consists of panretinal laser photocoagulation to induce regression of the neovessels and prevent recurrent hemorrhages.

items.title: Neovascular glaucoma
items.contentHtml:
Complication: neovascular glaucoma

  • Neovascular glaucoma is the most serious complication of ischemic central retinal vein occlusion, appearing between 2 and 3 months later (100-day glaucoma).
  • It is formed by neovascularization of the iris (rubeosis iridis) and the chamber angle, which obstructs the drainage of aqueous humor, raising intraocular pressure to values >40 mmHg.
  • Urgent management includes topical and systemic ocular hypotensive agents, and panretinal photocoagulation to induce regression of the neovessels; In advanced cases, filtering surgery or cyclophotocoagulation is required.

items.title: Intravitreal anti-VEGF
items.contentHtml:
Acute treatment: intravitreal anti-VEGF

  • Intravitreal injection of anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) is the first-line treatment for macular edema associated with retinal vein occlusion.
  • The approved drugs are ranibizumab (0.5 mg), aflibercept (2 mg) and bevacizumab (1.25 mg, off-label use); They are administered on a monthly loading regimen for 3-6 months, then as needed.
  • Visual response is evaluated with visual acuity and optical coherence tomography; A favorable response is considered if visual acuity improves ≥15 ETDRS letters.

items.title: laser photocoagulation
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: laser photocoagulation

  • Laser photocoagulation is indicated in branch retinal vein occlusion to treat macular edema (grid laser in the leak area) and to prevent retinal neovascularization (panretinal laser).
  • In central retinal vein occlusion, panretinal photocoagulation is reserved for cases with neovascularization of the iris or retinal, since it does not improve visual acuity in macular edema.
  • The argon laser (green or yellow) is applied with parameters of 200-500 µm, duration 0.1-0.2 s, sufficient power to produce mild retinal whitening; It should not be applied to the foveal area.
